exploration_log.aha_moment: After fopen_s succeeds and fd_ is set, call fchmod(fileno(fd_), mode_bits) to set the file permissions. fileno() gets the underlying file descriptor from the FILE*, and fchmod() sets its permissions. Guard with #ifndef _WIN32 and only call when mode_bits != 0.
exploration_log.hindsight: Could store mode_bits as a member and re-apply in reopen(), but the task only asks for open().

alternatives: Use chmod() on filename after close+reopen
key_decisions.decision: Call fchmod(fileno(fd_), mode_bits) immediately after fopen_s succeeds
key_decisions.reasoning: fchmod operates on the open file descriptor which is available right after open; using filename would require an extra system call and has race condition potential; inline in open() is cleanest

search_sequence.result: Found open() uses fopen_s; after success fd_ is set; fchmod(fileno(fd_), mode_bits) is the POSIX way to set permissions on an open file

search_sequence.action: Checked common.h for platform detection macros
search_sequence.reasoning: Needed to understand how to conditionally compile for POSIX vs Windows

solve_notes: Added an optional `mode_t mode_bits = 0` parameter to `file_helper::open()` on non-Windows platforms (guarded by `#ifndef _WIN32`). After `fopen_s()` succeeds and `fd_` is set, calls `::fchmod(::fileno(fd_), mode_bits)` when `mode_bits != 0`. Added `#include <sys/stat.h>` in the header for the `mode_t` definition.
task_text:
Fix file_helper::open not setting file permissions on POSIX

`file_helper::open()` in `details/file_helper.h` uses `std::fopen`
to create log files, inheriting the process umask. There is no way
to specify file permissions for newly created log files. Add an
optional `mode_t` parameter to `open()` that calls `fchmod` after
file creation on POSIX platforms to set the desired permissions.

tier_difference_reasoning: The minimum set covers the two edited definitions (open() in both header and inl). The thrash_preventing set is empty because the fix is contained in these two files, the POSIX API (fchmod/fileno) is standard, and no other spdlog definitions need to be read to implement the change.
